stem: From [[n]] distinct books, [[r]] of them are to be selected and placed in a specific order on a shelf. How many different ordered arrangements are possible?
solution: Order matters and there is no repetition, so the count is \( {}_{[[n]]}P_{[[r]]} = \frac{[[n]]!}{([[n]]-[[r]])!} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( \binom{[[n]]}{[[r]]} \), which treats the arrangement as unordered.
This divides by \( [[r]]! \) instead of \( ([[n]]-[[r]])! \).
This allows repetition, counting \( [[n]]^{[[r]]} \) sequences.
This arranges all [[n]] books rather than only [[r]] of them.
This is only the denominator \( ([[n]]-[[r]])! \) of the permutation formula.
This is off from the correct count by [[n]].

stem: From [[n]] distinct runners, [[r]] of them will finish in order to take medal positions 1st through [[r]]th. If a particular runner, Amy, is required to finish in exactly [[p]]th place, in how many ways can the OTHER [[r]]-1 medal positions be filled (in order) by the remaining runners?
solution: Amy's place is already fixed, so the remaining \( [[r]]-1 \) medal positions are filled, in order, from the other \( [[n]]-1 \) runners: \( {}_{[[n]]-1}P_{[[r]]-1} = \dfrac{([[n]]-1)!}{([[n]]-[[r]])!} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This ignores the requirement that Amy's place is fixed and counts all orderings of [[r]] positions from [[n]] runners.
This removes one extra runner from the pool beyond just Amy.
This treats the remaining assignment as an unordered selection rather than an ordered one.
This is only the denominator of the permutation formula, missing the numerator factorial.
This is off from the correct count by [[n]].
This is off from the correct count by [[r]].

stem: In how many distinguishable ways can [[n]] distinct people be seated around a circular table, if two seatings that differ only by a rotation of the whole table are considered the SAME arrangement?
solution: Fix one person's seat to eliminate rotational duplicates, then arrange the remaining \( [[n]]-1 \) people in the remaining seats: \( ([[n]]-1)! = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats the seats as a straight row, forgetting to divide out rotational duplicates.
This divides the row count by 2 instead of by [[n]] (confusing circular symmetry with reflection symmetry).
This also divides out reflections, which the problem did not ask to treat as identical.
This fixes two seats instead of just one, over-reducing the count.
This is off from the correct count by one.
This is off from the correct count by one.

stem: In how many ways can [[n]] distinct books be arranged in a row on a shelf if two particular books, X and Y, MUST stand next to each other?
solution: Glue X and Y into a single block, giving \( [[n]]-1 \) items to arrange in \( ([[n]]-1)! \) ways, then multiply by 2 for the two internal orders of X and Y within the block: \( 2\cdot([[n]]-1)! = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This ignores the adjacency requirement and arranges all [[n]] books freely.
This forgets the factor of 2 for the two internal orders of the X-Y block.
This treats the glued block as reducing the item count by 2 instead of by 1.
This divides by 2 instead of accounting for the block correctly.
This is off from the correct count by one.
This is off from the correct count by one.

stem: In how many ways can [[n]] distinct books be arranged in a row on a shelf if two particular books, X and Y, must NOT stand next to each other?
solution: From all \( [[n]]! \) arrangements, subtract the \( 2\cdot([[n]]-1)! \) arrangements where X and Y ARE adjacent: \( [[n]]! - 2([[n]]-1)! = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to exclude any arrangements and counts all of them.
This is the count of arrangements where X and Y ARE adjacent, the complement of what was asked.
This forgets the factor of 2 when subtracting the adjacent cases.
This is an unrelated smaller factorial, not the complementary count.
This is off from the correct count by one.
This is off from the correct count by one.
